question: What was the MPC vote split in February 2026?
expected_answer: 5-4 to maintain Bank Rate at 3.75%; four members voted to reduce to 3.5%
source_quote: voted by a majority of 5-4 to maintain Bank Rate at 3.75%

question: What was Brent crude price cited in the March 2026 MPC minutes?
expected_answer: Over $100 per barrel, around 60% higher than at the time of the February Report
source_quote: The Brent crude spot price in the run-up to the MPC meeting on 18 March had been over $100 per barrel

question: What was the judgement on CPI inflation cited in the November 2025 MPC minutes?
expected_answer: CPI inflation was judged to have peaked; increase over the year was attributed to energy base effects and one-off factors such as food supply disruptions and administered prices; no additional second-round effects expected in the central projection
source_quote: CPI inflation was judged to have peaked. The increase in headline inflation over the course of this year had been accounted for by base effects in energy prices, as well as one-off factors

question: When did the MPC last cut Bank Rate before March 2026?
expected_answer: December 2025, reduced to 3.75% by a 5-4 majority
source_quote: voted by a majority of 5-4 to reduce Bank Rate by 0.25 percentage points, to 3.75%

question: What was the household saving ratio cited in the November 2025 MPR?
expected_answer: 10.8% in 2025 Q2 (up from a slight dip in Q1; had peaked at 11.7% in 2024 Q4)
source_quote: the household saving ratio rose to 10.8% in Q2, having fallen back a little in Q1

question: How did the February 2026 MPR near-term inflation projection differ from November 2025?
expected_answer: February 2026 showed a lower near-term inflation outlook than November; the Committee welcomed this but noted uncertainty about pass-through to wage and price-setting
source_quote: the lower near-term outlook for inflation was welcome, it remained to be seen how this would pass through into wage and price-setting

question: How did the MPC's language on inflation persistence change between November 2025 and February 2026?
expected_answer: In November 2025 four hold-voting members emphasised inflation persistence risks. By February 2026 this narrowed to three members (Greene, Lombardelli, Pill) while Bailey and Mann shifted to emphasising the lower near-term path and weaker-activity risks
source_quote: four members in this group ... placed greater weight on risks of persistence in inflation / three members in this group (Megan Greene, Clare Lombardelli and Huw Pill), a more prolonged period of policy restriction was likely to be warranted

question: Compare voting patterns across November 2025, December 2025 and February 2026 MPC meetings
expected_answer: November 2025: 5-4 to hold at 4%. December 2025: 5-4 to cut to 3.75%. February 2026: 5-4 to hold at 3.75%. Same four dissenters each time (Breeden, Dhingra, Ramsden, Taylor) favoured faster easing
source_quote: majority of 5-4 to maintain Bank Rate at 4% ... majority of 5-4 to reduce Bank Rate by 0.25 percentage points ... majority of 5-4 to maintain Bank Rate at 3.75%

question: How did the BoE's assessment of global risks evolve between July 2025 and December 2025 FSRs?
expected_answer: July 2025 focused on elevated term premia and volatility after the 2 April US tariff announcements. December 2025 shifted focus to two US corporate defaults highlighting risks in leveraged credit, rapid expansion of AI-sector debt financing, and rising public debt-to-GDP in advanced economies
source_quote: news on trade tariffs have been the primary driver of moves in financial markets / Two recent high-profile corporate defaults in the US have intensified focus on potential weaknesses in risky credit markets

question: What changed in Catherine Mann's policy stance between November 2025 and February 2026?
expected_answer: November 2025: held firm; inflation persistence scenario was her central case; emphasised wage and expectations risks. February 2026: still voted to hold at 3.75% but stated explicitly that 'the appropriate time for a cut in Bank Rate closer', moving towards easing
source_quote: The inflation persistence scenario is my central case / New analysis and current developments have moved the appropriate time for a cut in Bank Rate closer

question: What specific consumption weakness scenario did Box D in the November 2025 MPR describe?
expected_answer: An elevated saving rate persists rather than falling back to historical norms, reflecting household caution and a desire to rebuild wealth eroded by inflation; this would keep consumption weak
source_quote: If households seek to rebuild their wealth, that could keep the saving ratio high and consumption weak

question: What structural labour market changes did Clare Lombardelli highlight in November 2025?
expected_answer: Structural changes (referenced to Box F) may mean less slack in the economy than headline measures suggest, leading to more persistent inflationary pressures
source_quote: Structural changes in the labour market (Box F) may mean there is less slack in the economy, leading to more persistent inflationary pressures

question: What risks from US corporate defaults did the December 2025 FSR identify?
expected_answer: Two recent high-profile US corporate defaults demonstrated over-reliance on Credit Rating Agencies (ratings stayed stable until close to default); illustrated weaknesses in leveraged credit markets including high leverage, weak underwriting standards, opacity and complex structures; highlighted potential contagion via diverse but opaque exposures
source_quote: Two recent high-profile corporate defaults in the US have intensified focus on potential weaknesses in risky credit markets ... high leverage, weak underwriting standards, opacity, complex structures, and the degree of reliance on credit rating agencies

question: What was the MPC's assessment of second-round effects from the Middle East energy shock in March 2026?
expected_answer: MPC was alert to the increased risk of domestic inflationary pressures through second-round effects in wage and price-setting; the risk would be greater the longer higher energy prices persist; Committee stood ready to act if inflation threatened to stay above the 2% target
source_quote: alert to the increased risk of domestic inflationary pressures through second-round effects in wage and price-setting, the risk of which will be greater the longer higher energy prices persist

question: What asymmetric policy risk argument did Lombardelli make in November 2025?
expected_answer: Plenty of policy space remained to lower Bank Rate if required, whereas a policy reversal (cutting then hiking) would be costly for MPC credibility
source_quote: we have plenty of policy space to lower Bank Rate should it be necessary, while a policy reversal would be costly for the MPC's credibility

question: What was the central argument of Alan Taylor's 'last half mile' speech at Leeds in January 2025?
expected_answer: The UK has made it to the last half mile on inflation, but with the economy weakening it is time to get interest rates back toward normal to sustain a soft landing. Risks are more skewed to the downside; Taylor argues for a more activist, more rapid withdrawal of monetary policy restrictiveness
source_quote: we've made it to the last half mile on inflation, but with the economy weakening, it's time to get interest rates back toward normal to sustain a soft landing

question: What did Andrew Bailey's February 2025 Chicago Booth speech argue about the scope of central-bank liquidity provision?
expected_answer: Traditional central bank orthodoxy treats commercial banks as the sole conduit for liquidity provision, but changes in financial market structure since the GFC mean this assumption no longer reliably reaches all parts of the system in stress; central banks may need to update their operating frameworks to transact more broadly
source_quote: the banking system has a special place, as the conduit for the transmission of central bank policies ... during the operation of these arrangements, there were times when strains in terms of the efficiency of the liquidity flow were evident

question: What did Catherine Mann identify as the main driver of the UK's 'consumption gap' in her October 2025 speech?
expected_answer: Real consumption has flatlined since the pandemic while nominal spending returned to trend. The gap is driven in part by frozen tax brackets (around 65% of the real disposable income shortfall), employee national insurance contributions (about 13%) and higher interest payable. Consumers have been scarred by high and volatile inflation
source_quote: around 65% of the difference between real incomes and real disposable incomes is accounted for by taxes and 13% by employee national insurance contributions in Q2 2025

question: What did Swati Dhingra argue about trade fragmentation in her February 2025 Dow Lecture?
expected_answer: Historical parallels show trade openness followed by retrenchment; 'beggar-thy-neighbour' protectionist policies in the 1930s expanded domestic growth only by subsidising domestic production at the expense of consumption. For a small open economy, trade shapes living standards and productivity. A new 'slowbalisation' phase has consequences for monetary policy
source_quote: Nations engaged in protectionist policies, imposing tariffs, restricting imports, and depreciating their currency. These 'beggar-thy-neighbour' policies were intended to expand domestic growth by subsidising domestic production at the expense of domestic consumption

question: What did Dave Ramsden emphasise in his June 2025 labour market speech?
expected_answer: Uncertainty and unpredictability have risen sharply, most recently driven by trade policy; the MPC is enhancing its forecasting toolkit and use of scenarios to test policy robustness; baseline forecast has weak underlying GDP growth, a hump in inflation in 2025 from energy and administered prices, and a spare-capacity margin opening up to support disinflation with CPI returning to target in early 2027
source_quote: GDP growth from 2025 Q2 onwards is projected to be much weaker than the headline numbers for Q1 ... A gradual fall in headline inflation is projected through 2026 with inflation returning to the 2% target in early 2027

question: What did Sarah Breeden argue in her January 2025 Edinburgh speech about identifying shocks?
expected_answer: Attributing data news to the wrong shocks has material implications for monetary policy; real-time shock identification is hard and the MPC now draws on hard data, soft data, Agency intelligence and multiple models. Output gap estimates have been revised materially — the pandemic output gap was less negative than initially thought, and latest estimates are at the top of the MPC's range
source_quote: Attributing data news to the 'wrong' shocks can have material implications for our understanding of the evolution of the economy in the future, and therefore what we should do with monetary policy
